title: 20 Nehase — The Seven Youths of Ephesus & The Assembly
narrative:
Nehase

The Seven Youths (Sleepers) of Ephesus

St. Samuel the Prophet (Monthly Commemoration)

St. Lucius of Cyrene, Apostle of the Seventy

The twentieth day of Nehase honors the Seven Youths of Ephesus — the Sleepers — one of the most beloved miracle-narratives of the universal Church. Seven young Christians of the third century, fleeing the Decian persecution, took refuge in a cave on Mount Celion near Ephesus and fell into a miraculous sleep. They woke, by divine action, some two centuries later — during the reign of the Emperor Theodosius II, in the era when the Church had triumphed over paganism but was being troubled by doubts about the resurrection of the body. When they emerged, bewildered, into a world utterly changed, and the truth of their long sleep became known, the effect on the community was electric: here, in living bodies walking through the market of Ephesus, was the bodily resurrection made visible ahead of its time. They had slept in the era of persecution and woken in the era of triumph, bearing between them the entire arc of the Church's early history in their persons.

The Ethiopian Church honors the Seven Youths with particular warmth, partly because the cave-as-holy-place is a deep archetype in the Ethiopian spiritual landscape — the cave of the nativity, the cave of the desert fathers, the rock-hewn churches of Lalibela, the cliff sanctuaries of Tigray. The mountain cave into which the seven fled was not merely a hiding place but a bridal chamber of the Spirit: they went in as frightened young men and came out as witnesses to the resurrection. The fast of the body — which the Filseta community has just completed — is its own kind of cave: an enclosed, constricted, apparently bare space that turns out to be the precondition for a new emergence.

The monthly commemoration of St. Samuel the Prophet brings the Hebrew prophetic tradition into today's assembly — Samuel who heard his name called in the night and did not know at first that it was God calling, whose mother Hannah had wept barren tears before him and whose birth was itself a miracle of answered prayer. Samuel anointed kings and rebuked them; he was the hinge between the era of the judges and the era of the monarchy; he was the one who saw what God saw when the outward appearance was misleading. His discernment was the fruit of a lifetime of listening, beginning with that first night-call when he lay down in the temple and heard: 'Samuel, Samuel.' The faithful who have just kept the Filseta fast are being formed in this same school of listening.

St. Lucius of Cyrene, one of the Seventy Apostles, is mentioned in the Acts of the Apostles as a teacher and prophet in the Antiochene church, and tradition connects his Cyrenean origin with the Libyan diaspora that was already in close cultural contact with North Africa. If Lucius of Cyrene is to be identified with the Simon of Cyrene who carried the Lord's cross — a tradition held in some communities — then his presence in today's assembly connects the Assumption season to the Cross in the most direct possible way: the one who carried the wood of death may have become a builder of the community that announced eternal life. Whether or not the identification holds, Lucius stands as a representative of African Christianity in its earliest apostolic form, a witness that the continent's relationship to the Gospel is as old as the Gospel itself.
